turns.content: Why does the standard Mutex really feel so slow when I'm just trying to read a config value? It seems like holding that lock for even microseconds is unacceptable in tight loops.

That's the classic Java developer trap thinking about locks; Rust forces you to handle this differently. For read-mostory workloads, you can skip fine-grained locking entirely by using Arc<RefCell<T>> or AtomicUsize for simple counters instead of a global Mutex. If your data structure is complex and needs shared access, that's where lock striping shines with RCU-style patterns in Rust to avoid the overhead.
I get it about avoiding the lock, but what if I have a hot path where multiple threads are writing? Is there a better pattern than just using Arc<Mutex<T>> everywhere?

Exactly, that's when you need fine-grained locking or lock striping. Instead of one big Mutex for the whole map, you can split your data into chunks and assign each chunk to its own dedicated mutex. This way threads only contend on their specific section rather than fighting over a global lock. You might also consider using RCU patterns where readers have no lock at all while writers use a separate mechanism.
So I'm just going to replace my global Mutex with multiple smaller ones? And what about the RCU part, does that actually exist in Rust?

Yes, you can split your data structure into chunks and assign each chunk its own dedicated mutex. For the RCU pattern specifically, it's implemented in crates like rcore or by using spin locks for readers while writers hold a separate lock to avoid contention on read-mostory workloads.
Wait, so I can just use multiple small locks? What about the overhead of managing all these individual mutexes?

Managing many small locks adds complexity but reduces contention significantly. You might also consider using spin locks for readers while writers hold a separate lock to avoid contention on read-mostory workloads.
